honoree.name: G. Mennen Williams
honoree.summary: G. Mennen Williams was an American politician who served as the Governor of Michigan from 1949 to 1961. Born in 1911, he was a prominent figure in the Democratic Party and known for his distinctive green bow ties. After his gubernatorial service, he later served as a justice on the Michigan Supreme Court. He died in 1988, leaving a legacy of progressive leadership in the state.

honoree.name: Iron Brigade
honoree.summary: Strong Vincent was an American lawyer and Union Army officer during the American Civil War, born on June 17, 1837, in Erie, Pennsylvania. He is best known for his leadership at the Battle of Gettysburg, where he was mortally wounded on July 2, 1863. Vincent graduated from Harvard College and Harvard Law School before beginning his legal career. He died on July 7, 1863, in Gettysburg, Pennsylvania, and is remembered as a hero of the Civil War.

honoree.name: Robert Radden
honoree.summary: Sheriff Radden succumbed to injuries sustained one month earlier when he was struck by a vehicle. He had responded to a bar after receiving reports of a fight at the location. He was struck by the vehicle as he walked from his car to the bar. The injuries caused him to lapse into a coma, which he never recovered from. Sheriff Radden had served with the agency for 4 years. He was survived by his wife and two children.

honoree.name: Marine Corps League
honoree.summary: Jack Bergman is an American politician and retired United States Marine Corps lieutenant general born in 1947. He served as the U.S. Representative for Michigan's 1st congressional district since 2017. Bergman had a distinguished military career, rising to the rank of lieutenant general and serving as the commanding general of the Marine Corps Forces Reserve. He is a member of the Republican Party and has been involved in politics and military service.

honoree.name: Ronald W. Reagan
honoree.summary: Ronald Wilson Reagan was born on February 6, 1911, in Tampico, Illinois. He served as the 40th President of the United States from 1981 to 1989 after a career as a film and television actor. Before his presidency, he was the Governor of California and president of the Screen Actors Guild. He died on June 5, 2004, in Los Angeles, California, leaving a legacy as a conservative icon who helped shape the end of the Cold War.

honoree.name: Corporal Matthew Edwards
honoree.summary: Corporal Matthew Edwards was shot and killed while responding to reports of a burglary in progress at an apartment complex on Pine Street at approximately 6:00 am. He and his partner encountered a man in the parking lot fitting the description of the suspect. As Corporal Edwards was speaking to him, the subject suddenly produced a handgun and fatally shot the officer. Corporal Edwards' partner returned fire and wounded the suspect. The suspect was found guilty of first degree premeditated murder in November 2011 and was sentenced to life in prison without parole. Corporal Edwards had served with the Taylor Police Department for nearly six years. He is survived by his wife and two children.

honoree.name: Cesar E. Chavez
honoree.summary: Cesar Chavez was born on March 31, 1927, in Yuma, Arizona, and became a prominent American labor leader and civil rights activist. He co-founded the National Farm Workers Association, which later became the United Farm Workers, and led the successful Delano Grape Strike. His nonviolent activism improved working conditions for farmworkers and brought national attention to their struggles. He died on April 23, 1993, in San Luis, Arizona, leaving a lasting legacy in labor and civil rights history.

honoree.name: Pere Marquette
honoree.summary: Jacques Marquette was a French Jesuit missionary born on June 1, 1637, in Laon, France. He is best known for exploring the Mississippi River in North America alongside Louis Jolliet. Marquette established missions in present-day Michigan and Wisconsin, contributing significantly to the mapping of the Great Lakes region. He died on May 18, 1675, in what is now Illinois, leaving a lasting legacy in American history.

honoree.name: Trooper Jeffrey Werda
honoree.summary: Trooper Jeffrey Werda was killed in an automobile accident while responding to assist Saginaw County deputies who were involved in a pursuit at approximately 1:30 am. Several minutes after he notified dispatchers he was responding to assist, a motorist called 911 to report finding a crashed patrol car on Birch Run Road, just west of Moorish Road in Birch Run Township. Trooper Werda's patrol car had rolled over several times and he was ejected from the vehicle. He was transported to Covenant HealthCare Hospital where he was pronounced dead. Trooper Werda had served with the Michigan State Police for 11 years.

honoree.name: Auxiliary Lieutenant Dan Kromer
honoree.summary: Auxiliary Lieutenant Danny Kromer was struck and killed by a hit-and-run driver on I-94 as he assisted a disabled motorist. He was standing next to the disabled vehicle when an approaching car side-swiped it and struck him. He was transported to a local hospital where he died the following morning. The driver of the vehicle who struck him was apprehended. He was convicted of second-degree murder and sentenced to 67 years in prison. Auxiliary Lieutenant Kromer had served with the Taylor Police Department for 19 years.

She was survived by her husband, who was also a trooper with the Michigan State Police, parents, brother, and two sisters.

honoree.name: Underground Railroad
honoree.summary: George DeBaptiste was an African-American businessman, abolitionist, and civic leader born in 1815. He worked as a valet to President William Henry Harrison and later became a successful ferry operator in Detroit. DeBaptiste was a key figure in the Underground Railroad, helping fugitive slaves cross the Ohio River. He also served as a recruiter for the Union Army during the Civil War and was a prominent leader in Detroit's Black community.
honoree.placeOfBirth: Georgetown, District of Columbia, United States

honoree.name: Earl DeMarse
honoree.summary: Correctional Officer Earl DeMarse was stabbed to death by an inmate at the Marquette Branch Prison. The stabbing took place only 15 minutes into his shift at 6:15 a.m. The prisoner who stabbed him was sentenced to life in prison and transferred to a different facility, where he later stabbed and wounded another officer. Officer DeMarse was a United States Army Air Forces WWII veteran and had served with the Michigan Department of Corrections for 26 years. He was survived by his wife, daughter, son, two brothers, and a granddaughter. The Earl F. DeMarse Training Academy was named in his memory.

honoree.name: Trooper Larry Forreider
honoree.summary: Trooper Larry Forreider was shot and killed after he and his partner pulled over a car for defective tail lights at 2:30 a.m. on US 23, two miles north of Alpena, Michigan. Trooper Forreider approached the driver's side of the car, and his partner was on the passenger side. While talking with the three occupants, Trooper Forreider's partner observed a gun in the vehicle.  Before the troopers could have all the subjects exit the passenger side of the car, the driver retrieved a second gun and opened fire. Trooper Forreider was struck twice. As the three subjects were fleeing, Trooper Forreider returned fire, striking one of the subjects, who was apprehended at the scene. The other passenger, who had escaped from a Kentucky prison, was apprehended 90 minutes later. The driver fled the scene and committed suicide as officers closed in on him 11 hours later. The injured passenger was convicted of attempted murder and carrying a concealed weapon and sentenced to 25 years. The escapee turned state's evidence in return for immunity and was returned to Kentucky prison, where he was convicted for additional robbery crimes he had committed years prior. Trooper Forreider was a United States Air Force veteran and had served with the Michigan State Police for seven years. He was survived by his wife, two children, parents, two brothers, and three sisters.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Gun; Unknown type Offender: Committed suicide; 25 years

highways.name: Detective Lavern S. Brann Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Detective Lavern S. Brann
honoree.summary: Detective LaVern Brann was shot and killed while he and his partner were investigating a homicide that had occurred the previous month. The shooting occurred when the two went to an apartment complex at 140 Brewer Drive to interview a woman and her mother. Detective Brann went to the front of the apartment complex while his partner went to the back of the building. While in the rear, Detective Brann's partner heard gunshots fired from the front of the complex. When he ran to investigate, he was met by an armed man. The two exchanged gunfire, and the detective was shot in the leg as the suspect fled the scene. When backup officers arrived, they found Detective Brann shot in the stairwell of the complex. He was transported to Battle Creek Health Systems, where he died from his wounds. The suspect was convicted of Detective Brann's murder and sentenced to life in prison without parole. Detective Brann had served with the Battle Creek City Police Department for 20 years. He is survived by his wife and two daughters.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Shotgun; 12 gauge Offender: Sentenced to life

honoree.name: Peter A. Pettalia
honoree.summary: Peter Pettalia was an American politician and businessman born on August 28, 1962. He served as a member of the New York State Assembly, representing the 101st district. Pettalia was known for his contributions to local infrastructure and economic development in his region. He passed away on September 13, 2017, leaving a legacy of public service.

honoree.name: Martin Luther King Jr
honoree.summary: Martin Luther King Jr. was born on January 15, 1929, in Atlanta, Georgia, and died on April 4, 1968, in Memphis, Tennessee. He was a Baptist minister and the most prominent leader in the American civil rights movement from 1955 until his assassination. King advanced civil rights through nonviolence and civil disobedience, inspired by his Christian beliefs and the nonviolent activism of Mahatma Gandhi. He is historically significant for his role in the Montgomery Bus Boycott, the March on Washington, and helping to secure the passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965.
honoree.education: Morehouse College, Crozer Theological Seminary, Boston University

highways.name: Trooper Robert J. Mihalik Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Trooper Robert J. Mihalik
honoree.summary: Trooper Robert Mihalik was shot and killed while attempting to arrest a man for leaving a gas station without paying. He spotted the man on I-94 in St. Joseph shortly after the broadcast was put out and was able to stop him in a residential area. During the arrest, the suspect was argumentative and failed to cooperate. After getting one handcuff on the man, he suddenly turned and pushed Trooper Mihalik against the hood of the cruiser and obtained control of his service weapon. Trooper Mihalik took cover on the other side of the hood, but the man again began to yell and fired at Trooper Mihalik six times, striking him in the chin and twice in the chest. Trooper Mihalik was able to return fire with his backup weapon and wounded the suspect, who was arrested minutes later by backup officers. The suspect was convicted of first-degree murder and sentenced to life on July 15, 1985. Trooper Mihalik was a United States Army veteran and had served with the Michigan State Police for 11 years. He was survived by his wife and two sons. In June 2020, Red Arrow Highway was renamed Robert J. Mihalik Memorial Highway.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Officer's handgun Offender: Sentenced to life

honoree.name: Kenneth J. Moraska
honoree.summary: Patrolman Kenneth Moraska was shot and killed after responding to a domestic disturbance. When Patrolman Moraska arrived at the scene of the domestic dispute he reportedly found the front door locked and in an attempt to enter the building approached the rear door. As he approached the rear door of the house he was shot in the head with a hunting rifle. The suspect had been previously arrested by Patrolman Moraska for disorderly conduct. After shooting Patrolman Moraska, the man stole Patrolman Moraska's gun and led other officers on a 6-hour chase before being apprehended. On December 1st, 1971, the suspect, 23, was convicted of second-degree murder and sentenced to life in prison. As of October 5, 2022, he was still in prison. Patrolman Moraska was a U.S. Army veteran of the Vietnam War and had served with the Norway Police Department for two years. He was survived by his wife and child. In May 2017, Route 2 in Norway was renamed Kenneth J. Moraska Memorial Highway.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Rifle; .30-.30 Offender: Sentenced to life

honoree.name: Tpr. Steven B. Devries
honoree.summary: Trooper DeVries was shot and killed after stopping a suspect who had just robbed a bank in Niles, Michigan. During the stop the suspect shot him in the chest. Responding officers found that during the course of the stop Trooper DeVries had written the man's name and address on a note pad and arrested the man. The suspect, sentenced to life, escaped on March 21, 1975, and was captured by FBI agents in Los Angeles three weeks later. He escaped again in 1987. Later that same year he was shot and killed by deputies in Mississippi following another bank robbery.

He achieved fame for his victories in the Valley Campaigns of 1864 and his defeat at the Battle of Little Bighorn. Custer died on June 25, 1876, at the Battle of Little Bighorn in the Montana Territory.

honoree.name: Gary Priess
honoree.summary: Police Officer Gary Priess was killed when he was struck by a tractor-trailer while making a traffic stop on U.S. Highway 127 near the I-69 interchange. He was speaking with the driver of the vehicle he had stopped, when the tractor-trailer struck his cruiser, and then him. The driver of the tractor-trailer pleaded guilty to negligent homicide and was sentenced to 90 days in jail, two years probation, and 60 hours of community service. Officer Priess had served with the DeWitt Township Police Department for 18 years. He is survived by his wife and two young children.

honoree.name: Rosa Parks
honoree.summary: Rosa Louise McCauley Parks was born on February 4, 1913, in Tuskegee, Alabama. She was an American activist who became a pivotal figure in the Civil Rights Movement after refusing to give up her bus seat to a white passenger in Montgomery in 1955. Her arrest sparked the Montgomery Bus Boycott, a pivotal event that led to the desegregation of public transportation. She continued her activism for decades and is widely celebrated as the 'Mother of the Civil Rights Movement' until her death on October 24, 2005.

honoree.name: Eric Overall
honoree.summary: Deputy Sheriff Eric Overall was struck and killed by a suspect's vehicle as he attempted to deploy stop sticks at approximately 12:30 a.m. Officers began pursuing the vehicle in the parking lot of the Lapeer County Jail after the man-made threats against law enforcement. The pursuit traveled into Oakland County on I-69, where the vehicle exited on M-15. Deputy Overall had deployed stop sticks and had taken up a position well off the roadway. The driver of the vehicle intentionally drove off of the roadway and struck Deputy Overall. Pursuing officers immediately took the man into custody. In October 2019 the man was convicted of first-degree murder and in December 2019, he was sentenced to life without parole. Deputy Overall had served with the Oakland County Sheriff's Office for 22 years. He is survived by his wife and son. In November 2019, M-15 was renamed, between Clarkston and Genesee County, to Officer Eric Overall Memorial Highway.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Vehicular assault Weapon: Automobile Offender: Sentenced to life without parole

honoree.name: Trooper Craig A. Scott
honoree.summary: Trooper Craig Scott was shot and killed after stopping a vehicle for speeding in Ingham County. Unbeknownst to him, the two occupants of the vehicle had stolen it four days earlier. The driver produced a driver's license but failed to produce a registration for the vehicle. Trooper Scott attempted to radio in the information but could not contact the dispatcher. He then approached the driver again and placed him under arrest. As he walked the subject back to the patrol car, the passenger exited the vehicle, snuck up behind him, and shot him in the back three times at a range of three feet. The two men fled the scene but were apprehended in the neighboring county after stealing a car at gunpoint. Both men were sentenced to life in prison. Trooper Scott was a United States Army veteran who served with the Michigan State Police for four years. In September 2019, U.S. 127 from Jackson County to Ingham County's Exit 56 was renamed Trooper Craig A. Scott Memorial Highway.

honoree.name: Collin Rose
honoree.summary: Sergeant Collin Rose succumbed to a gunshot wound sustained the previous day while questioning a suspicious person near the university's downtown campus. At about 6:30 p.m., Sergeant Rose was in an area that had experienced numerous thefts from vehicles the previous day. He informed dispatchers that he was making contact with a suspicious person in front of 3650 Lincoln Street. During the encounter, Sergeant Rose attempted to detain the man, but the subject suddenly pulled out a gun and shot him in the head. Responding officers transported Sergeant Rose to the Detroit Receiving Hospital. At 5:45 p.m. the next day, he succumbed to his wound. The subject fled the scene but was arrested in March 2017 after shooting at three Detroit police officers who were attempting to make contact with him. Two officers were struck, and one was medically retired due to his wounds. The subject was charged with Sergeant Rose's murder in August 2017. The case was dismissed on Jan. 3, 2019, after the suspect was deemed mentally unfit to stand trial. Sergeant Rose had served with the Wayne State University Police Department for 5 1/2 years and served as a canine officer with the agency. He had previously served with the Richland Police Department and was a civilian cadet at the New Baltimore Police Department. He is survived by his fiancée, parents, and brother. He was posthumously promoted to the rank of Sergeant. Sergeant Rose was a member of the Police Unity Tour. In May 2018, the Rose Dog Park at 3701 Trumbull, Detroit, was dedicated in honor of Sergeant Rose. In June 2018, Lodge Freeway, M-10, was renamed Sergeant Collin Rose Memorial Highway.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Incident Date: Tuesday, November 22, 2016 Weapon: Handgun Offender: Mentally unfit to stand trial

honoree.name: Trooper Byron J Erickson
honoree.summary: Trooper Byron Erickson died from injuries sustained in a vehicle crash on July 2, 1993. The crash occurred during the pursuit of a felon who had stolen a vehicle. He suffered spinal cord injuries and was unable to recover. The subject was later apprehended and sentenced on multi-jurisdictional warrants. Trooper Erickson had served with the Michigan State Police for four and a half years. He was survived by his wife and son.

honoree.name: Aretha L Franklin
honoree.summary: Aretha Franklin was born on March 25, 1942, in Memphis, Tennessee, and died on August 16, 2018, in Detroit, Michigan. She was an American singer and songwriter widely known as the 'Queen of Soul'. Franklin was renowned for her powerful voice and influential recordings of songs like 'Respect' and 'Chain of Fools'. She is recognized as one of the best-selling music artists of all time and a cultural icon.

honoree.name: Trooper Rodger M Adams
honoree.summary: Trooper Rodger Adams was killed in an automobile accident on US-12 in Lenawee County. As he and his partner were en route to an accident call another vehicle crossed into his lane and struck their patrol car head-on. The driver of the other car had fallen asleep at the wheel.

honoree.summary: Today we give honor and respect to Deputy Heikkila in remembrance of the sacrifice he made 26 years ago.  A memorial highway in his name will be dedicated to him on M-120 in Twin Lake. God Bless his family and family of blue for knowing he will never be forgotten.  True warrior, Rest In Peace. highways.name: Patrolman Bobby Lynn Williams Memorial Highway

honoree.name: Patrolman Bobby Lynn Williams
honoree.summary: Patrolman Bobby Williams was shot and killed after stopping a vehicle on West Beecher Road in Adrian at 6:25 a.m. Three men were just involved in a robbery and kidnapping of a gas station attendant, and a description of the vehicle was broadcast to local areas. Patrolman Williams spotted the vehicle matching the description and pulled over the car. The driver shot him as he approached the vehicle. Patrolman Williams was able to return fire, striking a tire. The occupants abandoned the car and were apprehended a short time later. Patrolman Williams was transported to University Hospital in Ann Arbor, where he succumbed to his wounds. The shooter, 31, was sentenced to life without parole. A juvenile was sentenced to 5- 50 years. The third, 18, was sentenced to life without parole. In 2024, he was resentenced after laws were changed prohibiting adolescents from receiving life without parole sentences. He was granted parole. Patrolman Williams was a United States Army Vietnam War veteran and had served with the Adrian Police Department for two years. He was survived by his wife and son. A part of West Beecher Street, from South Main Street to South Madison Street in Adrian, was renamed the "Patrolman Bobby Lynn Memorial Highway."

honoree.name: Darryl M. Rantanen
honoree.summary: Trooper Darryl Rantanen was killed when the patrol car he was riding in was forced off the roadway during a pursuit of a stolen vehicle on U.S. 2 just west of M-69. During the pursuit, Trooper Rantanen's partner attempted to pass the vehicle. Before he could do so, the juvenile driver swerved into the patrol car, causing it to turn on its side and strike a tree. His partner was seriously injured in the crash. The 16-year-old was later arrested, convicted of manslaughter, intent to do great bodily harm, and sentenced to nine to 25 years in prison. Trooper Rantanen had served with the Michigan State Police for eight years. He was survived by his wife and four children. In 2021 a section of U.S. 2/U.S. 41 between Hyde and Bark River was designated as the Darryl M. Rantanen Memorial Highway.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Vehicular assault Weapon: Automobile Offender: Sentenced to 25 years

honoree.name: Officer Mason Samborski
honoree.summary: Public Safety Officer Mason Samborski was shot and killed while conducting an investigation following a traffic stop. He had stopped a juvenile subject, and when the juvenile did not have a license, he took him to the Rue Versailles Apartment Complex to drop him off with an adult. After arriving at the location, Officer Samborski realized that the subject had taken him to a friend's apartment and not his residence; he stated that he would take him to jail unless he took him to his home. The juvenile tried to flee, and a scuffle ensued. They fell down a flight of stairs, and the subject grabbed Officer Samborski's gun and shot him. The suspect fled the scene with Officer Samborski's service weapon and was apprehended the following day. The suspect was found guilty of first-degree murder on November 19, 2010. He was sentenced to life without parole on December 16, 2010. In 2016 the U.S. Supreme Court ruled that the defendants, who were juveniles at the time of their crime and were sentenced to life without parole, was cruel and unusual and ordered new sentences for all who were. In 2022, he was resentenced to 40 to 70 years. He is eligible for parole in 2050. Officer Samborski had served with the Oak Park Police Department for 4 years. He is survived by his wife, child, parents, brother, and sister.
honoree.incidentDescription: Cause: Gunfire Weapon: Gun; Unknown type Offender: Sentenced to life

honoree.name: Officer Scott Flahive
honoree.summary: Officer Scott Flahive was shot and killed after he had stopped a vehicle driving without lights at Beacon Boulevard near Park Avenue. The vehicle had been chased into the city limits after two inmates escaped from the Ottawa County Jail. As Officer Flahive approached the car, one of the escapees, who was lying in the back seat, shot through the rear window with a 30-06 rifle, striking Officer Flahive. He was transported to North Ottawa Community Hospital, where he succumbed to his wounds. The suspects were apprehended immediately, less than 30 minutes after escaping jail. The shooter had planned on dying in a hail of bullets if he was stopped, but responding officers were able to prevent that from happening. He was sentenced to multiple life sentences. The two accomplices were sentenced to 10 and 15 years. Officer Flahive had served with the Grand Haven Department of Public Safety for four years. He was survived by his parents and sister, who became a law enforcement officer in Nevada. In December 2019, a section of US 31 was renamed "Officer Scott Flahive Memorial Highway" in his honor. The Scott A. Flahive Memorial Scholarship Fund offers educational assistance to local students pursuing careers in the fields of law enforcement and/or criminal justice. In 1994, the city dedicated the Scott Flahive Memorial Park.

honoree.name: UAW Sitdown Strike
honoree.summary: Genora Johnson Dollinger was an American labor and civil rights activist born on October 20, 1913, in Flint, Michigan. She was a key organizer during the 1936-1937 Flint Sit-Down Strike, leading the Women's Emergency Brigade. Dollinger was a founding member of the Detroit chapter of the National Organization for Women and advocated for women's and civil rights. She died on October 17, 2010, in Detroit, Michigan, leaving a legacy of social justice activism.

honoree.name: Officer Leroy Imus
honoree.summary: Police Officer Leroy Imus was struck and killed by a hit-and-run driver while he was on a traffic stop on the Van Dyke Bypass south of 19 MIle Road at 1:00 am. The 30-year-old driver of the vehicle turned himself in 18 hours later and was charged with manslaughter. Officer Imus was a U.S. Navy veteran and had served with the Sterling Heights Police Department for five years. He is survived by his wife and four children.
